prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
One fascinating example is the German word "Schadenfreude", which literally translates to "damage-joy". It refers to the guilty pleasure or satisfaction derived from witnessing or hearing about someone else's misfortune, mistakes or failures. 

There's no single English word that precisely captures the nuanced concept of taking a somewhat dark, secret enjoyment from the problems of others. We would have to use a longer phrase to convey the same idea.

Culturally, Schadenfreude reflects an almost inherent human tendency to feel good when someone else messes up, even if we know it's not nice. It captures that momentary satisfaction when we accidentally see a stranger take a spectacular fall on the street. Or the glee we feel after a long-time rival is brought low and humiliated.

While we can certainly empathize with a German-speaker feeling this emotion, we don't have a clean equivalent in English. That's part of what makes Schadenfreude such an interesting example of a concept that seems to transcend language and culture despite the lack of a direct translation.
